title: Light and Water
wikitext:
[]: Light and Water?             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:04 am While doing a little private investigation of zen philosophy (as inspired by a rather long post by Xard in the "Logic Warrior" thread) I came across something rather interesting that might just explain what the hell Ritsuko was talking about in 23. Ritsuko wrote: "Yes, this is where she was born. The water and light that form Rei's subconscious must have a strong impression of this place."

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:09 am Um, well done? Although seriously, you had me until you brought up the whole 'accepting instrumentality' thing at the very end. The message of the show, dare I say, the entire emotional core, is about not running away, about opening yourself to others regardless of the pain. Although now that I think of it, there's doesn't seem to be any reason that we can't open ourselves to others as well as connect with them.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:16 am I'm with Mugwump on this one.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:18 am Mugwump speaks truly.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:22 am edited to answer that issue (I just found additional meanings to the trigram in question...) as well as to put in the screenshot since Zuggy's link is dead.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:58 am Interesting! I’d ruminated on Ritsuko’s quote, but I’ve never looked at it like this before… Ritsuko wrote: "Yes, this is where she was born. The water and light that form Rei's subconscious must have a strong impression of this place."

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Sun Aug 09, 2009 6:38 am In other words, Rei is a reflection of the moon? I like this development, it suits her well. Not as sure about the Zen undertones that you refer to, but definitely interested in what else there is to take from it. As for the phrase that was quoted, based on the above, I think the important part would be the impression the room has made on Rei's psyche and how that would influence her behaviors/actions. Not up to projecting this onto what happens in EoE at the moment, but at the very least, the bleak nature of that laboratory probably relates well to personality of Rei(II) throughout the series (although, not necessarily the other two Rei's as they weren't born there, hmm...).

[]:             Quote Post by LiLi » Sun Aug 09, 2009 7:46 am Ritsuko's line about light and water made an impression on me too... I do associate Rei with water and moonlight... or more specifically with the reflection of the moon on water. LiLi wrote: Maybe Rei thinks she's only a reflection of the moon on the water...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Aug 09, 2009 9:40 am This thread gets Jimbo's seal of "SUPER DEEP MEANING" approval: Seriously, this is awesome stuff. In the past, I've only been able to connect the Rei/Moon/Water symbolism to Anno's overall duality motif of which the dichotomies are Asuka/Sun/Lava; though, in the latter case, the connections are less insistent, repetitive, and intense because I don't think Anno had any deeper meanings attached to them beyond their basic and implied symbolic associations. However, with Rei being the series' ultimate symbolic and metonymic device it makes sense the associations would be stronger and extend beyond her connections with Yui/Lilith/mothers/instrumentality/Genesis/etc. ATM I'm not in a mentally alert enough state to address, debate, or dispute it in more detail, but this is definitely a thread I'm going to keep an eye and try to analyze the theory in more detail later.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sun Aug 09, 2009 11:27 am Ritsuko wrote: The water and light that form Rei's subconscious

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Aug 09, 2009 12:46 pm LiLi wrote: I think Rei as a reflection of the moon on water "clicked" for me due to other in-series elements like the (rather obvious) imagery in the Fly Me To The Moon ED

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sun Aug 09, 2009 1:14 pm I'll come to this threat - too - after I get time. I'll be finishing huge writeup on Mari today and tomorrow and then I'll shift to Rei shit... and then other random topics. I don't know if I have time to contribute here or to the other Rei threat whole week. Sorry guys (if you are awaiting my reply to that other topic that is)

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sun Aug 09, 2009 1:59 pm This is a lot cooler explanation for Rei's moon imagery than the "First Impact" thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sun Aug 09, 2009 2:42 pm AuraTwilight wrote: This is a lot cooler explanation for Rei's moon imagery than the "First Impact" thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Sun Aug 09, 2009 6:54 pm Merridian wrote: This might go without saying, but it's interesting that Ritsuko says water and light. Rei's definitely connected to the moon in just about every shot Anno can spare, but in this scene, it's pretty deliberate that Ritsuko avoids saying 'moon' or anything related to nighttime--it's just water and light that comprise her subconscious. From that angle, not only is the moon's reflection important, but just about any other reflection of light off the surface of water. Then again, that could leave things extremely open-ended; everything from Ep. 01's introduction of Sachiel to Fuyutuski's Tree Scene Flashback have light reflected off of water

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Mon Aug 10, 2009 3:24 pm Merridian wrote: (smart stuff about self-awareness, and:) As it is, Buddha Nature isn’t something that be attained when one actively pursues it...

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Tue Aug 11, 2009 8:39 am VoidEater wrote: Millions of monks and meditators would disagree. But it is true that enlightenment comes in an instant - sometimes preceded by vast amounts of preparation.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Sat Aug 15, 2009 2:28 am Just checking up on the I ching, its roots are taoist rather than zen. This poses a problem as the initial quote was zen in nature, and with the previous two posts, it certainly seems unlikely that instrumentality had anything to do with zen and enlightenment. Hrm... pushing further though, if anyone's found anything for the other people, this may be interesting to look at.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sat Aug 15, 2009 2:58 am Merridian wrote: There's a parable about a bandit/thief/somethingrather (I can't remember, it's been a LONG time) trying his best to attack the Gautama Buddha, and there's a lot more to it than this, but part of the parable I was told describes him running as hard as he could to catch up with the guy. Although the Buddha was just walking, it didn't matter HOW hard the bandit ran; he couldn't even get within several yards of him. It wasn't until he stopped in physical anguish and threw himself down on the dirt that he realized that the Buddha hadn't moved at all, and was right in front of him the whole time. That's what I meant by the fruitless action of pursuing Bodhi. The more I run towards it, the more I try to grab at it because I feel bad or inadequate or lustful, the farther removed from it and the more indulgent in my illusions do I become.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sat Aug 15, 2009 11:04 am UrsusArctos wrote: It wasn't that he was after Bodhi - he was essentially in a state where he would just have to keep killing and killing with no end in sight, and no redemption. The Buddha finally said, "I have stopped. When will you stop?" and the murderer converted and became a monk.
